title: The Real-Time Everything Request
context: You're in a product review meeting for the HR platform. The Head of Product has just returned from a conference where she saw demos of real-time analytics dashboards and is now convinced that the platform needs real-time everything — live headcount updates, instant payroll status, real-time absence tracking, and streaming compliance alerts. She's brought the CTO and the VP of Product along. The engineering lead looks worried. You're the architect and everyone's looking at you to assess feasibility.

questions.prompt: The Head of Product opens with: "I want our HR dashboard to be fully real-time. When a manager approves an absence request, the team calendar should update instantly. When payroll finishes processing, the finance dashboard should show it immediately. No more waiting until tomorrow morning for stale data." The CTO adds: "Our competitors are marketing real-time. We need to keep up." How should you respond?

options.text: Real-time is definitely achievable with modern event-driven architecture. We can use Event Grid and SignalR to push updates to dashboards as they happen. Let me scope this out.
options.explanation: You've accepted the premise without questioning whether everything actually needs to be real-time. Saying everything is achievable sets an expectation that every data point will update live — which is enormously expensive to build and operate. The key architectural question you've missed: what's the actual freshness requirement for each use case?

options.text: I want to make sure we build the right thing. When you say 'real-time,' what decision does someone make differently if the data is 5 seconds old versus 5 minutes old versus updated hourly? Let's walk through each use case: absence calendar, payroll status, compliance alerts. They probably have very different freshness requirements — and different requirements mean different architectures and costs.
options.explanation: This is the correct architectural response. 'Real-time' is one of the most expensive words in engineering. By asking what decision changes with latency, you force the room to articulate actual requirements rather than aspirational ones. Most 'real-time' requests, when examined, turn out to need 'fresh enough' rather than live-streaming. Absence calendars might need minutes, payroll status needs daily, and only compliance alerts might genuinely need seconds.

options.text: Real-time architecture is significantly more complex and expensive than batch. We'd need event streaming infrastructure, WebSocket connections, and a complete rethinking of our data pipeline. I'd estimate 6-9 months minimum. Are we sure we want to invest that?
options.explanation: While your cost concern is valid, leading with the difficulty and a scary timeline makes you sound like you're blocking rather than helping. You've also treated 'real-time' as a monolithic requirement, when in reality each use case has different latency needs. Start by decomposing the requirement, then match each component to the right solution.

questions.prompt: The Head of Product thinks about it and says: "OK fair point. For the absence calendar, managers need it within a few minutes. For payroll status, finance just needs to know by end of business day. But for compliance — like right-to-work expiry — we absolutely need real-time alerts." The VP of Product adds: "See, compliance is critical. We can't have someone working with an expired visa because the system didn't update." How should you respond?

options.text: Great, so we need three different approaches. For the absence calendar — a simple webhook or event that updates within minutes; that's straightforward with our existing infrastructure. For payroll status — a scheduled refresh every few hours or end-of-day batch, which is what we probably already have. For compliance alerts — that's the one that genuinely needs an event-driven trigger with immediate notification. We've gone from 'rebuild everything as real-time' to one small event-driven feature.
options.explanation: You've decomposed the monolithic 'real-time' requirement into three distinct solutions at three different points on the complexity spectrum. The absence update is near-real-time (simple event), payroll is batch (already exists), and compliance is the only genuinely time-critical feature. You've reduced the scope dramatically while fully addressing the business need. This is the core architect skill: matching solutions to actual requirements.

options.text: Since compliance is the critical one, I'd suggest we build a real-time event streaming platform that all three use cases can consume from. That way we invest once and get real-time capability across the board.
options.explanation: This is the 'build a platform' instinct that leads to over-engineering. You've just heard that two of three use cases don't need real-time, yet you're proposing to build infrastructure as if they all do. A streaming platform is expensive to build and operate. When only one use case (compliance alerts) actually needs it, building a platform is premature and unjustifiable.

options.text: The compliance case is interesting, but I'd want to understand the legal requirement more precisely. How quickly does the system actually need to flag an expiring right-to-work document? Is it truly 'within seconds' or is 'checked daily' sufficient for legal compliance?
options.explanation: While questioning the requirement further is sometimes appropriate, you've already gotten a clear answer: compliance alerts need to be immediate. Right-to-work expiry is a genuine legal risk. Continuing to push back on a requirement the business has clearly articulated makes you look like you're stalling. You have enough information to propose the solution architecture now.

questions.prompt: The engineering lead speaks up: "For the compliance alerts, I've been looking at Kafka. If we set up a Kafka cluster, we can stream all HR events through it and build real-time consumers for any use case. It would future-proof us for any real-time requirement that comes up." The CTO nods: "I like the idea of investing in infrastructure that scales." How should you respond?

options.text: Kafka is the industry standard for event streaming. If we're investing in real-time infrastructure, it's the right choice. We should size it for our expected throughput.
options.explanation: You've accepted both the tool choice and the premise without challenging whether a streaming platform is justified. Kafka is powerful but operationally expensive — it needs dedicated expertise, cluster management, and monitoring. For a single compliance alerting use case, it's like buying a freight train to deliver a letter.

options.text: I appreciate the forward-thinking, but let's right-size the solution to the actual requirement. For compliance alerts, we need an event trigger when a right-to-work document approaches expiry, and a notification to the relevant manager. That's an Azure Function triggered by a timer or a database change event, pushing a notification via a service bus queue. No Kafka cluster needed. If we genuinely get five more real-time use cases in the next year, we can re-evaluate — but let's not build infrastructure for hypothetical future requirements.
options.explanation: You've applied the 'what requirement eliminates the simple option?' test perfectly. The compliance alert use case needs a trigger and a notification — not a streaming platform. Azure Functions + Service Bus is the simple option that solves the actual problem. By explicitly naming 'hypothetical future requirements' as the driver for Kafka, you've exposed the real motivation (future-proofing) and challenged it constructively.

options.text: Kafka might be overkill, but we should consider Azure Event Hubs as a managed alternative. It gives us the streaming capability without the operational overhead of running our own Kafka cluster.
options.explanation: You've challenged the specific tool but not the underlying architecture. Whether it's Kafka or Event Hubs, you're still proposing a streaming platform for a use case that only needs a trigger and a notification. The right conversation is about the pattern, not the product. Don't let the tool discussion distract from the requirement analysis.

questions.prompt: The Head of Product circles back: "What about the dashboards though? Even if the data doesn't need to be truly real-time, I want the dashboard to feel modern and responsive. No more 'last updated: 6am this morning' labels. Can we at least make it look real-time?" How should you respond?

options.text: If we want the dashboards to feel real-time, we should implement WebSocket connections from the browser so data pushes to the dashboard automatically. It's a bigger change but it'll give that modern feel.
options.explanation: WebSockets are a sledgehammer for this requirement. The Head of Product wants the dashboard to 'feel' responsive, not to stream live data. WebSocket infrastructure adds complexity in load balancing, connection management, and reconnection logic. There are much simpler ways to make a dashboard feel fresh.

options.text: Absolutely. That's a presentation concern, not an infrastructure one. We can have the dashboard poll for updates every 30-60 seconds with a lightweight API call. When fresh data arrives, the UI animates the changes smoothly. The user sees the dashboard update regularly without stale timestamps, and we don't need any streaming infrastructure. If specific widgets need faster updates — like a live absence counter — we can add a SignalR connection just for those.
options.explanation: You've correctly identified this as a UX problem, not an infrastructure problem. A periodic poll with smooth animations gives the 'feels real-time' experience at trivial cost. You've also left the door open for SignalR on specific high-value widgets if needed, showing you're flexible without over-committing. This is right-sizing the solution to the actual human need.

options.text: We could cache the dashboard data with a 5-minute TTL and refresh in the background. That way the dashboard always shows recent data without hitting the source systems on every page load.
options.explanation: While caching is sensible for performance, you haven't addressed the actual request — the Head of Product wants the dashboard to visibly update, not just load fast. A cache with a 5-minute TTL still shows the same data for 5 minutes. You need to address the UX perception of freshness, which is about visible updates and smooth transitions.

questions.prompt: The CTO wraps up: "This has been helpful. But I have one more concern. Our competitors genuinely are building real-time pipelines. Are we going to fall behind if we take the simpler approach? I don't want to look back in a year and wish we'd invested in the infrastructure." How should you respond?

options.text: You're right to think about the future. Let's compromise — build the simple solutions now but architect them so we can swap in a streaming backbone later when we need it. We'll define event schemas upfront so the transition is smooth.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable but 'architect for future swap-in' is a form of premature abstraction. Designing event schemas and abstraction layers for a streaming backbone you don't need yet adds complexity now for a hypothetical later. The clean approach is to build the simple thing simply, and accept that if streaming is needed later, you'll refactor at that point — with much better information about actual requirements.

options.text: The competitors are marketing real-time, but let's look at what that actually means in practice. Most 'real-time' HR platforms are doing exactly what I'm proposing — periodic polling, event-triggered updates, and smart caching. The marketing says 'real-time' but the implementation is 'fresh enough.' Our approach isn't falling behind — it's doing what everyone actually does, without the overengineering. If genuine real-time use cases emerge, the event-driven patterns we're using make it straightforward to add streaming later. We're not closing any doors.
options.explanation: This is the right way to handle competitive anxiety. You've reframed the CTO's concern by pointing out that 'real-time' marketing rarely means true streaming — it usually means exactly the patterns you've proposed. You've reassured that the event-driven approach doesn't close doors. And critically, you've addressed the emotional concern (falling behind) with a rational analysis, without dismissing it.

options.text: I can't predict what we'll need in a year. What I can tell you is that building infrastructure we don't need today wastes budget and adds operational burden. Let's solve today's problems today and reassess quarterly.
options.explanation: While the principle is correct, the delivery is too blunt for a CTO expressing a strategic concern. Telling a CTO 'I can't predict the future' and 'solve today's problems today' sounds like you're dismissing their legitimate strategic thinking. You need to engage with the competitive concern and reframe it, not wave it away with engineering pragmatism.
